era_context: The Roman Republic is entering a period of relative stability after a decade of internal conflict. The Sertorian War has ended; Spartacus's revolt has been suppressed; the Third Mithridatic War continues but is moving toward conclusion. In 70 BCE, Pompey the Great and Marcus Licinius Crassus celebrate triumphs for their victories and are elected consuls together, marking a significant moment in Roman politics. The consulship of Pompey and Crassus will be marked by the passage of the Lex Aurelia, which restores power to the tribunes of the plebs. In China, the Han Dynasty under Emperor Xuan continues consolidation. Egypt remains under Ptolemy XII.

events.title: Triumphs of Pompey the Great and Marcus Licinius Crassus
events.description: Pompey the Great and Marcus Licinius Crassus celebrate triumphs in Rome for their victories in Hispania and against Spartacus, respectively. Pompey's triumph celebrates his conquest of Hispania and the end of the Sertorian War; Crassus's triumph celebrates the suppression of the slave revolt. The two generals, despite their rivalry, are elected consuls together for 70 BCE. Their joint consulship marks a significant moment in Roman politics and demonstrates the power of military success in the late Republic.
key_figures: Pompey the Great (Roman general and consul)
Marcus Licinius Crassus (Roman general and consul)
events.certainty_note: The triumphs of Pompey and Crassus in 70 BCE are well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Lex Aurelia: Restoration of tribunes' power
events.description: During the consulship of Pompey and Crassus, the Lex Aurelia is passed, restoring significant power to the tribunes of the plebs. This law reverses many of the restrictions placed on the tribunate by Sulla during his dictatorship (82–79 BCE). The restoration of tribunician power is a major political victory for the populares faction and marks a shift in the balance of power in the Republic away from the optimates. The law allows tribunes to hold higher office and to propose legislation without senatorial approval, significantly increasing their political influence.
key_figures: Lucius Aurelius Cotta (likely proposer of the law)
events.certainty_note: The Lex Aurelia and the restoration of tribunician power in 70 BCE are well-attested in multiple sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: -82-001 (Sulla's dictatorship and restrictions on the tribunate)

events.title: Lucullus continues campaign in Pontus and Armenia
events.description: Lucullus continues his campaign against Mithridates VI and his Armenian ally Tigranes II. After his victory at Tigranocerta in 71 BCE, Lucullus advances further into Armenia and Mesopotamia, extending Roman influence into the eastern Mediterranean. However, his army is becoming increasingly restless due to the length of the campaign and the distance from Rome. The war will continue for several more years, but Lucullus's political support in Rome is beginning to erode.
events.certainty_note: Lucullus's continued campaign in 70 BCE is well-attested in sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Cicero prosecutes Verres for extortion in Sicily
events.description: Marcus Tullius Cicero prosecutes Gaius Verres, a former governor of Sicily, for extortion and corruption. The prosecution is a landmark case in Roman law and demonstrates Cicero's oratorical skills. Cicero's speeches against Verres (the Verrines) are among the most famous examples of Roman oratory and are preserved in the historical record. The case results in Verres's conviction and exile, and enhances Cicero's reputation as a defender of justice and the rule of law.
key_figures: Gaius Verres (defendant, former governor of Sicily)
events.certainty_note: Cicero's prosecution of Verres in 70 BCE is well-attested, including in Cicero's own speeches (the Verrines), which are contemporary primary sources. The date is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact nature and scope of the Lex Aurelia is somewhat disputed in modern scholarship; some sources suggest it was a more limited restoration of tribunician power than others. Additionally, while Cicero's prosecution of Verres is well-attested, the exact date of the trial (70 BCE) is sometimes given as 70 or 69 BCE in different sources, though 70 BCE is the more widely accepted date. The historical record for 70 BCE is generally reliable but depends on later sources for most events.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 70 BCE is rich compared to earlier years, though still dependent on later sources. Cicero's speeches (the Verrines) are contemporary primary sources, providing direct evidence for the trial of Verres. Plutarch and Appian are the primary sources for other events, writing 150+ years after the events. The sources are generally favorable to Pompey and Crassus, and may underrepresent the perspectives of Verres or other opponents. The consulship of Pompey and Crassus is a turning point in Roman history, marking the beginning of the end of the Republic, though this significance is not apparent in the sources themselves. Archaeological evidence from Rome, Sicily, and the eastern Mediterranean provides some corroboration.

graph_edges.note: The prominence of Pompey and Crassus in 70 BCE leads to their later alliance with Caesar in the First Triumvirate (60 BCE)
The restoration of tribunician power contributes to the political instability that leads to the Civil War (49 BCE)
Lucullus's campaign continues until Pompey takes over command in 66 BCE
